fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund stretches across large and mid-cap companies and has a 31-year track record. Since you prefer simple, set-and-forget investing, this single fund can handle about half your portfolio without you needing to switch between sectors or market caps. It's stable and well-established.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This is an 'Aggressive Hybrid' fund meaning it mostly invests in stocks (over 65%) but also holds some safer bonds. It balances growth with stability and has been around for 26 years. It helps you earn from both stocks and bonds without managing two separate funds.

fund.name: Helios Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Large & Mid Cap funds invest in bigger, more stable companies while still capturing growth from mid-caps. This fund has a good 1-year performance and the Helios team has a strong reputation. It adds extra growth potential alongside your Flexi Cap fund.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Large Cap funds invest only in the biggest, most stable companies in India. This one has a solid 18-year track record. It's like the 'safer' side of your stock portfolio and is less likely to panic you when the market dips compared to smaller companies.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a Direct Mutual Fund account (free) on apps like Groww, Kuvera, or Coin. You don't need to visit an office. Step 2: In the app, find each fund by its AMFI code: 118955, 120251, 152941, and 118632. Step 3: For each fund, select 'Growth' option and start a monthly SIP. Step 4: Set up auto-debit from your bank account 2 days after your salary credit. Step 5: Use your existing ₹10 lakh corpus by doing a one-time lumpsum investment split in the same proportions (45%, 25%, 15%, 15%) across these four funds. Keep your ₹8 lakh liability money in a high-yield savings account or liquid fund for emergencies.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Confirm your SIPs are running and money is debited. Check that no more than ₹40,000 is tied up (4 months * ₹20,000) as per your emergency fund goal. Any excess can stay invested.

After 1 year: Log in to your portfolio. Compare the actual percentage in each fund against your target (45%, 25%, 15%, 15%). If one has grown too much (e.g., Flexi Cap is now 55%), you can move some of that profit to the other funds to rebalance. If markets were bad and one dropped too much, just stay calm — time is your friend with 18 years ahead.

After 3 years: Check if any fund's performance is consistently trailing its peers (check the app or website). If one has underperformed badly (e.g., two below average for 2 years in a row), consider switching that money to another fund in the same category.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, around your birthday, check if your fund mix has drifted too far from 45%/25%/15%/15%. If any category has grown or fallen by more than 10 percentage points from its target, sell a part of the winner and buy into the loser to get back to the original mix. This is called 'rebalancing' and keeps your risk steady. Don't do it more often — stick to a simple calendar reminder.
plan.rationale: At 42 with 18 years until retirement, you have enough time to ride out market ups and downs, but since you mentioned a past 'financial scare' and want simple, stable options, I haven't put all your money in risky small-cap stocks. Instead, I've built a mix: two main buckets for growth (Flexi Cap + Large & Mid Cap), one 'balanced' bucket (Equity & Debt), and a stable Large Cap base. This gives you exposure to stocks (which grow fast over 18 years) but keeps about 25% in safer bond-heavy options to cushion volatility. The goal is ₹2 Crore by age 60. Your ₹20,000/month SIPs, growing at a modest 11.8% average, will create about ₹74 Lakhs. Combined with your current ₹10 Lakhs (becoming ~₹70 Lakhs by year 18 if kept invested), you'll reach close to ₹80–90 Lakhs. To hit ₹2 Crore, you may need to contribute more (increase SIPs as your salary grows) or this is a partial step toward that goal. For kids' education, park that in a separate, dedicated SIP once needed.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20–30% in a bad year. It will hurt your portfolio value temporarily. But with 18 years to go, history shows these corrections recover over time. Don't panic-sell during markets; that's when you lose the most.

You have a 20% tax bracket. Equity funds are taxed at a flat 12.5% (with ₹1.25L exemption) if held over 1 year, which is very reasonable. However, because you are slightly below the 'safe zone' for tax-free growth (marginally so), I chose Aggressive Hybrid and Large & Mid Cap funds which are balanced yet equity-taxed. Avoid debt funds that would trigger your 20% full income tax on gains. But even here, your gains would be small relative to 18 years.

If your annual salary doesn't increase (unlikely), your ₹20,000 SIPs will become a smaller slice of your retirement pie. Plan to increase SIPs by 10% every year (sinking fund style) to keep up with inflation.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase your total monthly SIP by ₹2,000 each year (10% of your ₹20,000 base) to match expected salary growth. This will help you reach ₹2 Crore by age 60.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: All equity allocations are to actively managed funds with stock-picking mandates (flexi cap, large & mid cap, etc.). There are no passive/index instruments in the equity allocation.

turnover_score: The plan recommends annual rebalancing around the investor's birthday with a 10 percentage point drift threshold. This is relatively passive with infrequent tactical adjustments.

cost_emphasis_score: Costs are not a primary emphasis in the plan. While it mentions some tax efficiency considerations, fund selection appears to be driven by investment style and performance rather than expense ratios.

research_vs_cost_score: The plan emphasizes fund-specific research with detailed rationale about fund characteristics, track records, and investment approaches rather than favoring broad market exposure without individual fund analysis.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The strategy is designed for a long-term 18-year horizon with infrequent intervention, matching the investor's retirement timeline and emphasizing patience during market fluctuations.

portfolio_activeness_score: All equity funds are actively managed with no passive/index funds in the portfolio. The funds aim to deliver returns through active stock-picking rather than tracking indices.

pqs.reasoning: The plan is generally well-aligned with the investor's goals, life stage, and moderate risk appetite. The allocation across equity and hybrid funds suits the 18-year horizon, though there is room to improve diversification by incorporating more geographies or sectors. The rationale for fund selection is coherent and consistent, with clear explanations for each allocation. The plan demonstrates strong tax awareness by avoiding slab-taxed debt funds for a 20% tax-bracket investor and recommending equity-taxed options instead. However, it misses an opportunity to suggest ELSS funds for 80C tax savings since the investor is in the old regime. Overall, the plan is solid, simple, and appropriate for the investor's set-and-forget preference.
